A review of human reliability assessment methods for proposed application in quantitative risk analysis of offshore industries

Nilesh Ade, S. Camille Peres *
Mary Kay O’Connor Process Safety Center, Artie McFerrin Department of Chemical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, 77843-3122, USA

A R T I C L E   I N F O
Keywords:
Quantitative risk analysis
Offshore
Human reliability assessment
Human factors

A B S T R A C T
Issues related to Human Factors have emerged as the prolific cause for a multitude of offshore incidents. This has led to an increased emphasis on studying and quantifying human elements in this domain. A conventional method applied to evaluate the risk associated with offshore platforms under both drilling and operations is quantitative risk analysis (QRA). Human reliability analysis (HRA) approaches have been developed that incorporate human performance and the resulting human errors in QRA for a more holistic overview of the associated risks with offshore facilities. However, due to the complex socio-technical nature of the offshore industry, the effective integration of HRA in QRA has remained a challenge. In this study, we have reviewed various HRA methods applied in offshore domain along with various QRA methods that facilitate HRA integration. The study revealed that the offshore domain mainly utilizes first-generation HRA methods for QRA of offshore emergency scenarios and second-generation methods for normal design and operation. It was also observed that certain QRA methods were more compatible with specific HRA techniques depending on the nature of the HRA technique.

1. Introduction

The offshore oil and gas industry is regarded as a high-risk domain due to the associated hazards of exploration, drilling, storage and processing of hydrocarbons (Broni-Bediako and Amorin, 2010). This risk is reflected in the high likelihood and consequences associated with offshore incidents as observed in the period 1964–2015, wherein 2440 oil spills in the Gulf of Mexico amounted to over 12 million gallons of the oil spill (Howell, 2018). These statistics exclude the Deepwater Horizon incident that itself resulted in 130 million gallons of oil spill along-with 11 fatalities (The Ocean Portal Team, 2018). This incident was regarded as the largest oil spill in US history (Graham et al., 2011). Interestingly, even minor offshore incidents can lead to significant economic losses as such incidents can contribute to an increase in the downtime of the facility leading to a loss in production.

To mitigate the likelihood of such loss of life and economic resources, it is important to analyze risk methodically. The risk is defined as the product of the likelihood of an undesired scenario and the consequence associated with it (CCPS, 2000). The quantification of risk is traditionally achieved by quantitative risk analysis (QRA). Based on the assessment of the quantified risk, management of risk is achieved by improved design and operation of the facility (CCPS, 2000). However, quantitative risk analysis of offshore facilities is inherently a complex problem due to the socio-technical nature of these systems and to their dynamics. The design and operation of offshore facilities involves interactions between technical and human factors (Skogdalen and Vinnen, 2011). In complex industrial systems, it has been observed that around 80% of the incidents occur wholly or partly due to the way humans interact with the system—often referred to has human error (Baron, 1988). Human-system interactions have been identified as key contributors to incidents in the offshore industry as well (Gordon, 1998). One of the highlights from the incident investigation report of the Deepwater Horizon incident was the need to systematically identify and manage human’s interactions (both with technology and each other) within these sociotechnical systems (Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board, 2016):

“The committee recommends that MMS (Minerals Management Service) place its primary emphasis on detection of potential accident-producing situations, particularly those involving human factors, operational procedures, and modifications of equipment and facilities”.

Hence, an effective QRA for the offshore facility should account for all attributes of these socio-technical systems, i.e., the human, technical factors, and operational issues. Such integration may allow for accurate

A review of human reliability assessment methods for proposed application in quantitative risk analysis of offshore industries

Nilesh Ade, S. Camille Peres

Mary Kay O’Connor Process Safety Center, Artie McFerrin Department of Chemical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, 77843-3122, USA

A R T I C L E   I N F O

Keywords:
Quantitative risk analysis
Offshore
Human reliability assessment
Human factors

A B S T R A C T

Issues related to Human Factors have emerged as the prolific cause for a multitude of offshore incidents. This has led to an increased emphasis on studying and quantifying human elements in this domain. A conventional method applied to evaluate the risk associated with offshore platforms under both drilling and operations is quantitative risk analysis (QRA). Human reliability analysis (HRA) approaches have been developed that incorporate human performance and the resulting human errors in QRA for a more holistic overview of the associated risks with offshore facilities. However, due to the complex socio-technical nature of the offshore industry, the effective integration of HRA in QRA has remained a challenge. In this study, we have reviewed various HRA methods applied in offshore domain along with various QRA methods that facilitate HRA integration. The study revealed that the offshore domain mainly utilizes first-generation HRA methods for QRA of offshore emergency scenarios and second-generation methods for normal design and operation. It was also observed that certain QRA methods were more compatible with specific HRA techniques depending on the nature of the HRA technique.

1. Introduction

The offshore oil and gas industry is regarded as a high-risk domain due to the associated hazards of exploration, drilling, storage and processing of hydrocarbons (Broni-Bediako and Amorin, 2010). This risk is reflected in the high likelihood and consequences associated with offshore incidents as observed in the period 1964–2015, wherein 2440 oil spills in the Gulf of Mexico amounted to over 12 million gallons of the oil spill (Howell, 2018). These statistics exclude the Deepwater Horizon incident that itself resulted in 130 million gallons of oil spill along-with 11 fatalities (The Ocean Portal Team, 2018). This incident was regarded as the largest oil spill in US history (Graham et al., 2011). Interestingly, even minor offshore incidents can lead to significant economic losses as such incidents can contribute to an increase in the downtime of the facility leading to a loss in production.

To mitigate the likelihood of such loss of life and economic resources, it is important to analyze risk methodically. The risk is defined as the product of the likelihood of an undesired scenario and the consequence associated with it (CCPS, 2000). The quantification of risk is traditionally achieved by quantitative risk analysis (QRA). Based on the assessment of the quantified risk, management of risk is achieved by improved design and operation of the facility (CCPS, 2000). The design and operation of offshore facilities involves interactions between technical and human factors (Skogdalen and Vinnen, 2011). “The committee recommends that MMS (Minerals Management Service) place its primary emphasis on detection of potential accident-producing situations, particularly those involving human factors, operational procedures, and modifications of equipment and facilities”.

However, to achieve this required integration, it is essential to understand how humans interact with and within systems from the perspective of system safety. Causes of reduced human performance (i.e., human error) have been identified in multiple previous studies. Some of the well-known causes include inadequate risk perception (Patel et al., 2012), improper decision making (Manchi et al., 2013), and lack of situation awareness (Di et al., 2013)—all of these leading to negative impact on the safety performance of the system. Reason (1990) identified several classifications of human errors: slips (actions not carried as intended), lapses (actions missed), mistakes (inadequate plans leading to undesired outcomes irrespective of the actions) (Reason, 1990). Cumulatively, human error is defined as the unintended failure of achieving the planned outcomes in a form of action, checking, retrieval, transmission, diagnostic and decision errors (Alkhaldi, M, Pathirage, C and Kulatunga, 2017). However, it is important to understand that these errors can originate from multiple levels: the individual, group, and organizational. Interestingly, the level of the human factor is determined by the nature of human error. This relationship between levels of the human factor and the nature of human errors (Gordon, 1998) has been explained through the Rasmussen’s skill, rule, knowledge (SRK) (Rasmussen, 1993) and is shown in Table 1: The incorporation of HRA in QRA has been a subject of interest for offshore domain (Skogdalen and Vinnem, 2011). A study focusing on 15 QRAs applied in Norway and UK offshore revealed that many of these methods did not sufficiently address HRA (Skogdalen and Vinnem, 2011). It was also observed that these QRA methods varied substantially thus making the derived results questionable. To address these gaps, the goal of this study is aimed to evaluate the HRA methods from the perspective of their application towards offshore QRA. To achieve this objective, it is essential to understand the origins of HRA methods, their classifications and their current applicability in offshore domain. A review pertaining around these elements might provide insights into appropriate selection of HRA methods for QRA applications based on specific type of offshore tasks (e.g. maintenance). Similarly, the review will reflect the current state of the HRA based QRA methods, the improvements needed in these methods, the origins for variations (i.e. uncertainties) and directions for future studies. 2. Method To achieve the mentioned goals, current literature was reviewed and potential methods for achieving this goal were identified. These methods were identified from various safety journals and conference proceedings. The safety journals included those considered in a recent study that tried to evaluate the relative ranking of 35 safety journals based on multiple aspects such as expert opinion and citations (Genserik Reniers and Yannick Anthone, 2012). Conference proceedings from various well-known safety conferences such as offshore technology conference (OTC), research partnership to secure energy for America (RPSEA), Mary Kay O’Connor process safety center international symposium (MKOPSC), offshore oil and gas producers (OOGP), American Institute for chemical engineers (AIChE), center for offshore safety (COS), center for operator performance (COP), international association for drilling contractors (IADC) and society for petroleum geophysicists (SPG) were reviewed as well to address the proposed research objective. To identify relevant articles amongst these journal articles and conference proceedings a combination of sets of keywords were used, similar to that proposed in a previous review study (Goerlandt et al., 2017). The first set of keywords were related to QRA and included “QRA”, “risk analysis”, “risk assessment”, “safety”, “safety assessment”. The second set of keywords were related to human factors and included “human”, “human factor”, “human error”, “human and organizational factor”. These two sets of keywords were used along with the term “offshore” to limit the search in the offshore domain. Lastly, using the indexing system of Ei-Compendex, more relevant articles were added based on the citations (references) of identified articles. The resulting sets of articles were manually screened considering the review scope and defined research goals. The manual screening was done based on the title, abstract and full text (if readily available and the information in the abstract was limited). 2.1. Traditional methods for human factor integration in QRA In an effective QRA, the contribution of both technical and human factors towards risk is evaluated. Specifically, human factors contribute largely to the likelihood element of risk whereas technical factors contribute to both the consequence and likelihood element of risk (CCPS, 2000). The first step in QRA is defining the undesired scenario for which the risk has to be evaluated (Crowl and Louvar, 2011). This is followed by identifying the hazards in the process, defining likely scenarios, initiating and intermediate events in those and possible adverse outcomes. Human errors are mainly involved as either initiating or intermediate events in such scenarios (CCPS, 2000). The likelihood of human errors (as initiating or intermediate events) is conventionally evaluated through human failure databases, human reliability analysis (HRA) (Steijn et al., 2017) or human error estimation (CCPS, 2000). Human error estimation is often done using expert judgment or based on arbitrary rules (Steijn et al., 2017) and therefore, the overall QRA results deduced based on such estimation are questionable due to the associated uncertainty. The use of failure databases for estimation of human error likelihood is deemed superior to the mentioned human error estimation approach as the human failure data is generally collected through laboratory studies, field studies and expert judgment or a combination of these methods and is, therefore, equally or more practicable (CCPS, 2000). However, these databases are afflicted with uncertainties due to variations in the type of tasks, environmental conditions, quality of

The first step in QRA is defining the undesired scenario for which the risk has to be evaluated (Crowl and Louvar, 2011). This is followed by identifying the hazards in the process, defining likely scenarios, initiating and intermediate events in those and possible adverse outcomes. Human errors are mainly involved as either initiating or intermediate events in such scenarios (CCPS, 2000). The likelihood of human errors (as initiating or intermediate events) is conventionally evaluated through human failure databases, human reliability analysis (HRA) (Steijn et al., 2017) or human error estimation (CCPS, 2000). Human error estimation is often done using expert judgment or based on arbitrary rules (Steijn et al., 2017) and therefore, the overall QRA results deduced based on such estimation are questionable due to the associated uncertainty. The use of failure databases for estimation of human error likelihood is deemed superior to the mentioned human error estimation approach as the human failure data is generally collected through laboratory studies, field studies and expert judgment or a combination of these methods and is, therefore, equally or more practicable (CCPS, 2000). However, these databases are afflicted with uncertainties due to variations in the type of tasks, environmental conditions, quality of

However, to achieve this required integration, it is essential to understand how humans interact with and within systems from the perspective of system safety. Causes of reduced human performance (i.e., human error) have been identified in multiple previous studies. Some of the well-known causes include inadequate risk perception (Patel et al., 2012), improper decision making (Manchi et al., 2013), and lack of situation awareness (Di et al., 2013)—all of these leading to negative impact on the safety performance of the system. Reason (1990) identified several classifications of human errors: slips (actions not carried as intended), lapses (actions missed), mistakes (inadequate plans leading to undesired outcomes irrespective of the actions) (Reason, 1990). Cumulatively, human error is defined as the unintended failure of achieving the planned outcomes in a form of action, checking, retrieval, transmission, diagnostic and decision errors (Alkhaldi, M, Pathirage, C and Kulatunga, 2017). However, it is important to understand that these errors can originate from multiple levels: the individual, group, and organizational. Interestingly, the level of the human factor is determined by the nature of human error. This relationship between levels of the human factor and the nature of human errors (Gordon, 1998) has been explained through the Rasmussen’s skill, rule, knowledge (SRK) (Rasmussen, 1993) and is shown in Table 1: The incorporation of HRA in QRA has been a subject of interest for offshore domain (Skogdalen and Vinnem, 2011). A study focusing on 15 QRAs applied in Norway and UK offshore revealed that many of these methods did not sufficiently address HRA (Skogdalen and Vinnem, 2011). It was also observed that these QRA methods varied substantially thus making the derived results questionable. To address these gaps, the goal of this study is aimed to evaluate the HRA methods from the perspective of their application towards offshore QRA. To achieve this objective, it is essential to understand the origins of HRA methods, their classifications and their current applicability in offshore domain. A review pertaining around these elements might provide insights into appropriate selection of HRA methods for QRA applications based on specific type of offshore tasks (e.g. maintenance). Similarly, the review will reflect the current state of the HRA based QRA methods, the improvements needed in these methods, the origins for variations (i.e. uncertainties) and directions for future studies. 2. Method To achieve the mentioned goals, current literature was reviewed and potential methods for achieving this goal were identified. These methods were identified from various safety journals and conference proceedings. The safety journals included those considered in a recent study that tried to evaluate the relative ranking of 35 safety journals based on multiple aspects such as expert opinion and citations (Genserik Reniers and Yannick Anthonie, 2012). Conference proceedings from various well-known safety conferences such as offshore technology conference (OTC), research partnership to secure energy for America (RPSEA), Mary Kay O’Connor process safety center international symposium (MKOPSC), offshore oil and gas producers (OOGP), American Institute for chemical engineers (AIChE), center for offshore safety (COS), center for operator performance (COP), international association for drilling contractors (IADC) and society for petroleum geophysicists (SPG) were reviewed as well to address the proposed research objective. To identify relevant articles amongst these journal articles and conference proceedings a combination of sets of keywords were used, similar to that proposed in a previous review study (Goerlandt et al., 2017). The first set of keywords were related to QRA and included “QRA”, “risk analysis”, “risk assessment”, “safety”, “safety assessment”. The second set of keywords were related to human factors and included “human”, “human factor”, “human error”, “human and organizational factor”. These two sets of keywords were used along with the term “offshore” to limit the search in the offshore domain. Lastly, using the indexing system of Ei-Compendex, more relevant articles were added based on the citations (references) of identified articles. The resulting sets of articles were manually screened considering the review scope and defined research goals. content.description: This is a hierarchical flowchart illustrating how performance shaping factors (PSFs) influence human error probability in diagnostic tasks. The chart categorizes tasks into 'Experience' and 'Ergonomics', with each factor having two levels: 'High' and 'Low' for experience, and 'Misleading', 'Poor', and 'Good' for ergonomics. It quantifies the impact of these factors on human error probability, showing that high experience reduces the error rate to 0.5%, while low experience increases it to 3%. Ergonomics also significantly affects error rates, with misleading conditions leading to a 50% error rate, poor ergonomics resulting in a 10% error rate, and good ergonomics returning the error rate to 0.5%.

controls and displays, motivation, stress, skill, training, and quality of instructions (Meister, 1978; NRC, 1998). The HRA approach for human error likelihood method is the widely recommended approach (Bell and Holroyd, 2009) wherein qualitative and quantitative methods are used for human error identification, quantification, and reduction. These methods fully/partially account for the mentioned variations and therefore, the deduced QRA results from these methods are more plausible (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). Interestingly, some of the HRA methods are completely/partially based on expert judgment and human failure databases. An example of a commonly used HRA method is shown in Fig. 1: The HRA methods are generally classified as first-generation, second-generation and third-generation methods (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). In first-generation methods, humans are treated equivalently to mechanical components in the sense that humans are assumed to have natural flaws that result in the logical failure of tasks (Di et al., 2013). The first-generation methods are characterized by indirect treatment of context, the absence of a cognitive model, focus on the phenomenology of human actions, the binary representation of human performance (success/failure) and emphasis on quantifying the likelihood of incorrect performance (Kim, 2001). They are generally based on skill and rule-based errors and therefore, ignore context and organizational human factors. Examples of these methods include a technique for human error rate prediction (THERP) (Swain and Guttmann, 1983), human cognition reliability (HCR) (Madonna et al., 2009) and accident sequence evaluation program (ASEP) (Kim and Jung, 2003). The second-generation methods were developed to counter the deficiencies of the first-generation method. Specifically, these methods are based on cognitive modeling. They are focused on the causes of human errors as opposed to their frequency and emphasize the interactions of factors that lead to these errors (Iannone et al., 2004). These factors are referred to as performance shaping factors (PSFs) i.e. factors related to the environment, human (operator) and task that has the potential to cause human error. Essentially, PSFs in first generation methods focus only on the operator characteristics whereas, PSFs in second-generation methods focus on the task and environment characteristics as well. Examples of second-generation methods include cognitive reliability and error analysis method (CREAM) (Hollnagel, 1989) and standardized plant analysis risk–human reliability analysis method (SPAR-H) (Boring and Blackman, 2007). However, even these methods suffer from certain deficiencies: lack of empirical data for model development and validation, inaccurate cognitive modeling, variability in implementation and reliance on expert judgment (Griffith and Mahadevan, 2011). Lastly, to overcome these drawbacks of second-generation methods, third-generation methods were developed. An example of these methods includes nuclear action reliability assessment (NARA) (Kirwan et al., 2004). These methods are based on specific databases and therefore, the results derived from these methods have relatively lower uncertainties (Di et al., 2013). Overall, in recent years, there has been an increased emphasis on PSFs and their contribution towards HRA as seen in the generational advancement of these methods. However understanding the interactions and dependencies amongst PSFs has proven to be a challenge towards HRA (Di et al., 2013). It is also important to note that ambiguities exist with respect to classification of HRA methods amongst the three generations (De Oliveira et al., 2017). The general notion of generations amongst the HRA methods is primarily used by the HRA community to describe chronology of development as opposed to their underlying methodologies. However, for the specific goals of this study described previously, the above described definitions of the 3 generations will be followed. 2.2. HRA methods implemented in offshore industries Although HRA methods are extensively used in offshore industries, they were primarily developed for the nuclear domain. Specifically, HRA was developed for probabilistic risk assessment of nuclear weapons assembly in the 1960s (Cross and Youngblood, 2017). Subsequently, HRA was adapted for risk assessment for nuclear power plants and was introduced in the nuclear regulatory framework through a first-generation HRA method; THERP (Fleming, 1974; Swain and Guttmann, 1983). Currently, HRA is used in various safety-critical domains such as aerospace, rail, transportation and oil, and gas. A study by the UK health and safety executive identified 17 HRA methods that were of potential use to major hazard authoritative organizations (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). These methods included first, second and third-generation methods. Interestingly, a similar study was carried by the international association of oil and gas producers (IOGP) to identify and propose HRA methods that were suitable for oil and gas QRA (IOGP, 2010). Based on this study, a standard referred as IOGP 434-5 was put forth that recommended the application of 3 specific HRA methods. The 17 identified methods by UK-HSE, their generation, brief description, applicable domains and their presence/absence in the IOGP 434-5 standard is shown in Table 2. As observed from Table 2, most HRA methods were developed for the nuclear domain. Although both nuclear and offshore domain are similar in the sense that both are high-risk domains with the potential to cause incidents with severe consequences to personnel and environment, HRA methods developed for a nuclear domain cannot be directly applied for offshore applications because of certain inherent differences between these domains. These differences include highly centralized control nature of nuclear power plants, the involvement of exploration, extraction, transportation and refining activities in offshore, the difference in the principle technology and differences in magnitude and types of hazards involved (Boring, 2015). In order to counter these differences and develop an HRA method for oil and gas domain, the Norwegian oil industry and Norwegian research council undertook the Petro-HRA

model), HEART and THERP. Specifically, SLIM and SLIM-based HEPI were implemented for emergency scenarios as the SLIM method is inherently based on a ‘what-if’ approach which makes it applicable for such scenarios (Khan et al., 2006). SLIM utilizes expert judgment to determine human error probabilities (HEP). The SLIM model was successfully implemented to determine HEP for offshore platform musters (DiMattia et al., 2005). Specifically, the model considered 3 muster scenarios: man overboard, gas release and fire and explosion. 24 experts provided their judgments in the form of weights and ratings for six PSFs: stress, complexity, training, experience, event factors and environment factors. This data was then converted into HEP using SLIM protocol (DiMattia, 2004). Another SLIM-based model, HEPI was subsequently developed and validated for the Ocean Odyssey case study (Khan et al., 2006). HEPI utilized the same six PSFs from the offshore muster study. However, the HEPI model differed from the core SLIM model in the sense that HEPI utilized SI reference graphs to determine HEP. Also, the model utilized a risk matrix comprising of consequence severity and HEP to evaluate the overall risk. The application of reference graphs in HEPI facilitated standardization of the SLIM model and the implementation of risk matrix provided insight in the relation between HEP and human factors such as training, design, safety systems, and procedures. Another emergency scenario; offshore evacuation was analyzed using the HEART method (Deacon et al., 2013). However, in this study along-with expert judgment, learnings from incident investigations are also used for HEP calculation. However, limited application of the first-generation method for modeling non-emergency scenarios was observed in this review. This included an integrated approach consisting of SLIM and THERP for HRA during maintenance activity in an offshore platform (Abbassi et al., 2015). It is important to note that offshore maintenance is a transient operation as well and therefore the ‘what-if’ applicability is also suitable for such operation.

In a general context, first-generation HRA methods have been observed to only focus on external behavior and errors while ignoring the cognitive reasons and mechanisms behind these errors (Sträter et al., 2004). Specifically, these methods give less importance to the environment, organizational factors and other relevant factors (Hollnagel, 1989; Mosleh and Chang, 2004; Strater et al., 2004). Moreover, the absence of a standardized method for accounting expert judgments makes the results deduced from these methods questionable. These deficiencies of first-generation methods lead to higher estimates of HEP with wide uncertainty bounds (Hollnagel, 1989).

2.4. Second-generation HRA methods in offshore industries

Although SLIM and HEPI proved to be applicable for the offshore domain, they were mainly designed/applied for emergency scenarios. A study was conducted to reevaluate HRA methods and identify methods that were appropriate for normal design and operation (Gould et al., 2012). This study served as a cornerstone for evaluation of HRA methods from the perspective of the offshore domain. As a part of this study, HEART, CREAM, and SPAR-H models were tested for the offshore domain. It was observed that both HEART and CREAM suffered from low interrater reliability, unreliable results (i.e. unreliable error probabilities) and were too complicated to apply. It was concluded from this study that SPAR-H method was the most applicable method for the offshore domain as it is non-resource intensive, provides specific guidance on the assignment of weights to PSFs and provides standard worksheets that promoted consistency. SPAR-H being a second-generation HRA method is based on cognitive modeling. It is specifically based on explicit information processing model of human performance wherein the perception processing response is assumed to depend on information inflow, task demand characteristics, environmental and situational factors, external, short-term and long-term memory (Blackman et al., 2008; Boring and Blackman, 2007). However, due to its nuclear origin, SPAR-H was still found to be not fully applicable to oil and gas domain. The method requires differentiating diagnosis and action tasks which is quite complex in the offshore domain. Also, the PSF for ‘available time’ in SPAR-H is calibrated for nuclear control room tasks and might be inaccurate for offshore tasks such as drilling that deal with higher time-scales. These deficiencies in SPAR-H served as motivation for the development for Petro-HRA, a SPAR-H based HRA method optimized for petroleum domain (Bye et al., 2016).

Petro-HRA method broadly consists of scenario definition,

content.description: This is a quadrant diagram illustrating the interdependencies of operator characteristics, administrative and organizational characteristics, process characteristics, and task characteristics in a technical system. Each quadrant lists specific factors that influence these categories, such as competence, fatigue, work environment, and procedures, work permit, and work practice for the respective quadrants. The diagram visually represents how these factors collectively impact the overall performance and safety of the system.

Specifically, SLIM and SLIM-based HEPI were implemented for emergency scenarios as the SLIM method is inherently based on a ‘what-if’ approach which makes it applicable for such scenarios (Khan et al., 2006). SLIM utilizes expert judgment to determine human error probabilities (HEP). The SLIM model was successfully implemented to determine HEP for offshore platform musters (DiMattia et al., 2005). Specifically, the model considered 3 muster scenarios: man overboard, gas release and fire and explosion. 24 experts provided their judgments in the form of weights and ratings for six PSFs: stress, complexity, training, experience, event factors and environment factors. This data was then converted into HEP using SLIM protocol (DiMattia, 2004). Another SLIM-based model, HEPI was subsequently developed and validated for the Ocean Odyssey case study (Khan et al., 2006). HEPI utilized the same six PSFs from the offshore muster study. However, the HEPI model differed from the core SLIM model in the sense that HEPI utilized SI reference graphs to determine HEP. Also, the model utilized a risk matrix comprising of consequence severity and HEP to evaluate the overall risk. The application of reference graphs in HEPI facilitated standardization of the SLIM model and the implementation of risk matrix provided insight in the relation between HEP and human factors such as training, design, safety systems, and procedures. Another emergency scenario; offshore evacuation was analyzed using the HEART method (Deacon et al., 2013). However, in this study along-with expert judgment, learnings from incident investigations are also used for HEP calculation. However, limited application of the first-generation method for modeling non-emergency scenarios was observed in this review. This included an integrated approach consisting of SLIM and THERP for HRA during maintenance activity in an offshore platform (Abbassi et al., 2015). It is important to note that offshore maintenance is a transient operation as well and therefore the ‘what-if’ applicability is also suitable for such operation.

In a general context, first-generation HRA methods have been observed to only focus on external behavior and errors while ignoring the cognitive reasons and mechanisms behind these errors (Sträter et al., 2004). Specifically, these methods give less importance to the environment, organizational factors and other relevant factors (Hollnagel, 1989; Mosleh and Chang, 2004; Strater et al., 2004). Moreover, the absence of a standardized method for accounting expert judgments makes the results deduced from these methods questionable. These deficiencies of first-generation methods lead to higher estimates of HEP with wide uncertainty bounds (Hollnagel, 1989).

2.4. Second-generation HRA methods in offshore industries

Although SLIM and HEPI proved to be applicable for the offshore domain, they were mainly designed/applied for emergency scenarios. A study was conducted to reevaluate HRA methods and identify methods that were appropriate for normal design and operation (Gould et al., 2012). This study served as a cornerstone for evaluation of HRA methods from the perspective of the offshore domain. As a part of this study, HEART, CREAM, and SPAR-H models were tested for the offshore domain. It was observed that both HEART and CREAM suffered from low interrater reliability, unreliable results (i.e. unreliable error probabilities) and were too complicated to apply. It was concluded from this study that SPAR-H method was the most applicable method for the offshore domain as it is non-resource intensive, provides specific guidance on the assignment of weights to PSFs and provides standard worksheets that promoted consistency. SPAR-H being a second-generation HRA method is based on cognitive modeling. It is specifically based on explicit information processing model of human performance wherein the perception processing response is assumed to depend on information inflow, task demand characteristics, environmental and situational factors, external, short-term and long-term memory (Blackman et al., 2008; Boring and Blackman, 2007). However, due to its nuclear origin, SPAR-H was still found to be not fully applicable to oil and gas domain. The method requires differentiating diagnosis and action tasks which is quite complex in the offshore domain. Also, the PSF for ‘available time’ in SPAR-H is calibrated for nuclear control room tasks and might be inaccurate for offshore tasks such as drilling that deal with higher time-scales. These deficiencies in SPAR-H served as motivation for the development for Petro-HRA, a SPAR-H based HRA method optimized for petroleum domain (Bye et al., 2016).

These deficiencies in SPAR-H served as motivation for the development for Petro-HRA, a SPAR-H based HRA method optimized for petroleum domain (Bye et al., 2016).

Petro-HRA method broadly consists of scenario definition,

qualitative data collection, task analysis, human error identification, modeling, quantification, and reduction (Bye et al., 2016). However, a distinguishing feature of Petro-HRA is its non-linear nature as shown in Fig. 3. Petro-HRA involves iterations within and between steps throughout the method to ensure robust analysis. This method is specific for the petroleum industry in the sense that it provides context-specific guidance on qualitative data collection and analysis, quantitative analysis, and HRA integration in QRA. However, Petro-HRA has its own limitations. The Petro-HRA method has been designed for post-initiator scenarios but incidents can occur from pre-initiator events such as maintenance errors. In a simpler scene, human error may be contributing cause for an incident (pre-initiator) as opposed to a response to an incident (post-initiator). The Petro-HRA method has not been tested and validated for such pre-incident scenarios. More importantly, the method is designed for control room tasks and therefore, the PSFs for this method must be adjusted appropriately for non-control room tasks.

In comparison to first-generation methods, second-generation methods such as SPAR-H/Petro-HRA try to explain explicit behavior through cognitive modeling. These methods explain the interdependencies amongst the PSFs and interaction amongst these factors that lead to human errors (Iannone et al., 2004). However, many of the second-generation methods lack theoretical or experimental basis (Di et al., 2013). Specifically, from a quantitative perspective, most of these methods involve implicit relationships between PSFs and HEP (Mosleh and Chang, 2004). The second-generation methods have not been empirically validated as opposed to first-generation methods that have been largely validated (Kirwan, 1996, 1997; Kirwan et al., 1997). Lastly, it is important to note that ambiguities exist with respect to the classification of SPAR-H method. Past studies mention SPAR-H as a first-generation method (Alvarenga et al., 2014; Bell and Holroyd, 2009; Garg et al., 2017) due to its reliance on nominal human error probabilities to predict human reliability. However, several other studies (Di et al., 2013; Wang, 2013; Yao, 2013) classify it as a second-generation method owing to its reliance on cognitive factors for estimating those human error probabilities.

2.5. Third-generation HRA methods in offshore industries

Despite being based on cognitive modeling, the second-generation methods suffer from severe drawbacks. These drawbacks include lack of empirical data for model development and validation, inadequate human behavior modeling, large variability in implementation, heavy reliance on expert judgment in the selection of PSFs and their implementation (Kim et al., 2006). These drawbacks can be countered by the development of third-generation methods. These methods rely on methodological tools based on specific human reliability databases to reduce uncertainty in HRA (Di et al., 2013). In a simpler sense, third-generation methods are improved versions of first-generation methods with industry-specific data that is valid and reproducible. However, currently only a single third-generation method exists; NARA (Refer Table 2) which is based on the databases: human event repository and analysis (HERA) (Halbert and Gertrmann, 2004) and the human factors information system (HFIS). It is important to note that, this method has only been implemented for the nuclear domain yet and has not been adapted for the offshore domain. Moreover, NARA is a British Energy proprietary tool and is not available publicly.

content.description: This is a flowchart depicting the Petro-HRA (Petrochemical Human Reliability Analysis) method steps for assessing human error in industrial processes. The chart outlines seven sequential stages: scenario definition, qualitative data collection, task analysis, human error identification, human error modeling, human error quantification, and human error reduction. It also illustrates the inputs and outputs of the method, including QRA (Quantitative Risk Assessment), recommendations for installation, and the integration of human factor engineering with QRA. The flowchart visually represents the structured approach to identifying, quantifying, and mitigating human errors within petrochemical operations.

2.6. HRA integration in offshore QRA

This review study revealed various studies that effectively integrated

![Petro-HRA method steps diagram](page_1012_1042_484_484.png)

Fig. 3. Petro-HRA method (Bye et al., 2016).

HRA with QRA. However, The HRA methods that were integrated were found to be mainly first and second-generation methods. These HRA methods were not the well-known methods described in Table 2. The QRA methods that were used to incorporate these HRA methods include safety barrier analysis (SBA) (Sklet et al., 2006a), Bayesian network (BN) (Musharraf et al., 2013), hybrid causal logic (HCL) (Roed et al., 2009) and functional resonance analysis method (FRAM) (Toroody et al., 2016).

One of the earlier methods for HRA-QRA integration for the offshore domain was the development of barrier and operational risk analysis model (BORA) (Sklet et al., 2006a, 2006b). The BORA model utilizes a second-generation HRA approach with SBA to perform a qualitative and quantitative risk analysis of the platform-specific hydrocarbon release frequency. The BORA model broadly consists of release scenario modeling, safety barrier performance modeling, risk quantification, identifying, weighing and scoring of PSFs and adjustment of risk for the specific platform under consideration. The PSFs used in this model involves the characteristics of the human (operator), task and environment thus, making the method second-generation. Overall the model consists of 27 PSFs. However PSFs used for a specific risk study are selected on a case-by-case basis and their weighing is carried through expert judgment. BORA is advantageous in the sense that it helps in understanding the effect of technical, human, operational and organizational PSFs on various safety barriers and overall risk of the system. Thus, BORA can also be used to study the effect of risk-reducing measures on release frequency. One of the major disadvantages of this model is its barrier-centric nature of HRA that may overlook pre-initiator events (Boring, 2015) similar to Petro-HRA.

Another major disadvantage of the BORA model is that it assumes that PSFs have independent effects on the basic events of a scenario. Thus, this model neglects the interaction effects amongst PSFs towards basic events for a scenario (Sklet et al., 2006b). These interaction effects can be effectively modeled using BN. The BN approach can provide a relatively simple and updateable model that can accommodate uncertainty in the relationships between PSFs. Dynamic BN has been successfully applied for QRA in offshore blowouts (Cai et al., 2013). In this second-generation Dynamic BN approach, human error is described using human factor barrier failure comprising of individual factor barrier failure, organizational factor barrier failure, and group factor barrier failure. This approach was successful in studying the effect of 37 PSFs and the interactions amongst them. A key finding from this review is the research mapping of HRA and HRA-based QRA that can help in the selection of appropriate HRA and QRA methods. Based on the findings of this review study, a selection path has been put-forth and shown in Fig. 4.

2.7. Limitations of current HRA-QRA models and recommendations for future research

This review study provided key insights into the current deficiencies of HRA models applied in the offshore domain. The first-generation methods inherently rely on skill and rule-based human errors (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). These methods assign nominal HEPs based on the characteristics of the tasks through the effect of PSFs. However, due to this approach, the effect of task characteristics is more profound than that of context. In the offshore domain, negligence of context can lead to inaccurate HRA/QRA results. An example of the importance of context in offshore HRA is the study that focused on the effect of a cold environment on HRA (Noroozi et al., 2014). One context in the offshore domain can be the physical environment of the facility. The mentioned study used a revised HEART technique to study the effects of cold (Arctic) environment on the HRA of post-maintenance tasks of offshore oil and gas pumps. The study revealed that the value of risk associated with the task is twice as high as that when the task is done in normal conditions. Another drawback of first-generation methods is that they do not consider the effect of characteristics of the task through PSF and account for their effect only through nominal HEPs (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). This drawback was highlighted in a study focusing on the HRA of offshore drilling activities wherein PSFs relevant to task was utilized in a second-generation HRA and these PSFs were observed to have a significant impact on the consistency of HRA results (Strand and Haskins, 2018).

The key difference between first-generation HRA methods and second-generation HRA methods is that the prior is behavior based models whereas the later are the conceptual type (Chang and Mosleh, 2007). The second-generation methods are based on the principle that human failures (or errors) are predominantly of cognitive nature (Hollnagel, 1989). It was observed in this review study that, the implementation of second-generation HRA methods for QRA was on a much smaller scale as compared to the first-generation methods. One of the possible reasons for this observation can be that the first-generation HRA methods are simpler to implement and require less empirical data due to more reliance on expert judgment. Another contributing reason can be that first-generation methods have been largely validated

2.7. Limitations of current HRA-QRA models and recommendations for future research

This review study provided key insights into the current deficiencies of HRA models applied in the offshore domain. The first-generation methods inherently rely on skill and rule-based human errors (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). These methods assign nominal HEPs based on the characteristics of the tasks through the effect of PSFs. However, due to this approach, the effect of task characteristics is more profound than that of context. In the offshore domain, negligence of context can lead to inaccurate HRA/QRA results. An example of the importance of context in offshore HRA is the study that focused on the effect of a cold environment on HRA (Noroozi et al., 2014). One context in the offshore domain can be the physical environment of the facility. The mentioned study used a revised HEART technique to study the effects of cold (Arctic) environment on the HRA of post-maintenance tasks of offshore oil and gas pumps. The study revealed that the value of risk associated with the task is twice as high as that when the task is done in normal conditions. Another drawback of first-generation methods is that they do not consider the effect of characteristics of the task through PSF and account for their effect only through nominal HEPs (Bell and Holroyd, 2009). This drawback was highlighted in a study focusing on the HRA of offshore drilling activities wherein PSFs relevant to task was utilized in a second-generation HRA and these PSFs were observed to have a significant impact on the consistency of HRA results (Strand and Haskins, 2018).

The key difference between first-generation HRA methods and second-generation HRA methods is that the prior is behavior based models whereas the later are the conceptual type (Chang and Mosleh, 2007). The second-generation methods are based on the principle that human failures (or errors) are predominantly of cognitive nature (Hollnagel, 1989). It was observed in this review study that, the implementation of second-generation HRA methods for QRA was on a much smaller scale as compared to the first-generation methods. One of the possible reasons for this observation can be that the first-generation HRA methods are simpler to implement and require less empirical data due to more reliance on expert judgment. Another contributing reason can be that first-generation methods have been largely validated

HRA with QRA. However, The HRA methods that were integrated were found to be mainly first and second-generation methods. These HRA methods were not the well-known methods described in Table 2. The QRA methods that were used to incorporate these HRA methods include safety barrier analysis (SBA) (Sklet et al., 2006a), Bayesian network (BN) (Musharraf et al., 2013), hybrid causal logic (HCL) (Roed et al., 2009) and functional resonance analysis method (FRAM) (Toroody et al., 2016).

One of the earlier methods for HRA-QRA integration for the offshore domain was the development of barrier and operational risk analysis model (BORA) (Sklet et al., 2006a, 2006b). The BORA model utilizes a second-generation HRA approach with SBA to perform a qualitative and quantitative risk analysis of the platform-specific hydrocarbon release frequency. The BORA model broadly consists of release scenario modeling, safety barrier performance modeling, risk quantification, identifying, weighing and scoring of PSFs and adjustment of risk for the specific platform under consideration. The PSFs used in this model involves the characteristics of the human (operator), task and environment thus, making the method second-generation. Overall the model consists of 27 PSFs. However PSFs used for a specific risk study are selected on a case-by-case basis and their weighing is carried through expert judgment. BORA is advantageous in the sense that it helps in understanding the effect of technical, human, operational and organizational PSFs on various safety barriers and overall risk of the system. Thus, BORA can also be used to study the effect of risk-reducing measures on release frequency. One of the major disadvantages of this model is its barrier-centric nature of HRA that may overlook pre-initiator events (Boring, 2015) similar to Petro-HRA.

Another major disadvantage of the BORA model is that it assumes that PSFs have independent effects on the basic events of a scenario. Thus, this model neglects the interaction effects amongst PSFs towards basic events for a scenario (Sklet et al., 2006b). These interaction effects can be effectively modeled using BN. The BN approach can provide a relatively simple and updateable model that can accommodate uncertainty in the relationships between PSFs. Dynamic BN has been successfully applied for QRA in offshore blowouts (Cai et al., 2013). In this second-generation Dynamic BN approach, human error is described using human factor barrier failure comprising of individual factor barrier failure, organizational factor barrier failure, and group factor barrier failure. This approach was successful in studying the effect of 37 PSFs and the interactions amongst them. It is interesting to note that the basic event and relation probabilities of the developed BN are based on expert judgment. In a similar study, BN was combined with Swiss cheese model for QRA of offshore platforms (Ren et al., 2008). This model comprises of 5 levels that describe the causal chain of safety assessment: root cause level, trigger level, incident level, accident level, and consequence level. Expert judgment is used in this study as well to determine the probability of nodes in BN. However, the application of Swiss cheese model reduces the complexity of the model and subsequently, the amount of data required through expert judgment. In a similar effort to reduce the dependence on subjective expert judgment, evidence theory was combined with BN for QRA of an offshore emergency evacuation (Musharraf et al., 2013). Evidence theory allows the combination of judgments from multiple experts thus, reducing the associated uncertainty. Interestingly, the results from this study were compared with SLIM; a method that relies heavily on expert judgment and the results were found to be similar.

BN are primarily used to study the interrelationships between different causes that can lead to an undesired scenario through calculation of posterior probabilities and therefore require the causal structure leading to a scenario (Alizadeh et al., 2014). Fault trees are applied for generating the causal structure itself. HCL are models comprising of BN based on fault trees and are, therefore, able to determine the contribution of various factors towards final failure (Wang et al., 2011). HCL model has been successfully applied for HRA integration in QRA of offshore platforms (Roed et al., 2009). This second-generation approach is itself based on the BORA model with the main difference being the application of HCL as opposed to SBA. More importantly, the HCL model has simpler scoring of PSFs as compared to the BORA model while being more flexible by allowing causal relationships through several PSF layers.

SBA, BN, and HCL were therefore successful in integrating second-generation HRA methods for QRA in the offshore domain. With respect to first generation methods, it was observed that FRAM could be utilized for the application of an HRA integration with offshore QRA. The FRAM approach is suitable for complex socio-technical systems as it describes such systems in the form of system functions. Each function is described by six parameters: input, output, time, control, precondition and resource (Hollnagel, 2004). The mentioned study was implemented for the application of QRA of the lifting of light structures in the sea with a crane. SLIM being a first-generation approach, this study mainly focused on PSFs relevant to skill and knowledge (individual human factors) and relied on expert-judgment for HEP calculation. The integration of a first-generation method (such as SLIM) within FRAM is effective as FRAM can develop a high risk scenario by considering the non-linear socio-technical interactions in a system and the human error probabilities associated with the high risk scenario can be accurately evaluated using the database reliant binary/logical workflow of first-generation methods.

A key finding from this review is the research mapping of HRA and HRA-based QRA that can help in the selection of appropriate HRA and QRA methods. Based on the findings of this review study, a selection path has been put-forth and shown in Fig. 4.

content.description: This is a flowchart illustrating the selection process for Human Reliability Analysis (HRA) and Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) methods based on the type of offshore activity. It categorizes activities into emergency/transient operations and normal design and operation, guiding through first-generation and second-generation methods, with specific tools like SLIM/THERP, FRAM, SPAR-H, SBA, BN, and HCL highlighted as applicable techniques.

Type of offshore activity Generation of method preferred Applicable HRA methods Applicable QRA methods
Emergency/Transient operations First-generation SLIM, THERP FRAM
Normal design and operation Second-generation SPAR-H (Petro-HRA) SBA, BN, HCL
Fig. 4. Selection of appropriate HRA and QRA methods for offshore. through experiments which are not the case for second-generation methods (Kirwan, 1996, 1997; Kirwan et al., 1997). Since first-generation methods provide less importance to task and environmental factors, they are associated with higher HEP estimates with wider uncertainties (Hollnagel, 1989) and therefore, it is important to improve current second-generation methods from the perspective of empirical validation. As second-generation methods essentially deal with cognitive modeling for human-system integration, their empirical validation can be achieved with human experimentation (Blanchard and Fabrycky, 2006). An ideal future direction for research can be the design of human experiments for cognitive models implemented in these methods. Specifically, such methods should deal with the effects of different environmental and task PSFs on various offshore activities. Another approach can be the development of second-generation methods that require reduced quantity of data. An example of such effort is the BN model developed through node reduction algorithms for the application of HRA (Zwirglmaier et al., 2017). The developed model can capture the causal paths in cognitive psychology and therefore provides traceability in HRA. An interesting observation of this review study was identifying that currently, no third-generation methods exist for the offshore domain. Though, it is important to note that only a single third-generation method exists for the nuclear domain as well (Table 2). In a simpler sense, third-generation methods are models that are based on specific human reliability databases and therefore have reduced uncertainties associated with them. However, establishing human reliability databases for an offshore domain that relate multiple PSFs relevant to the offshore domain (Fig. 2) is difficult. Another challenge is that relevant PSFs vary substantially with respect to tasks and environment. A control room task in a normal environment will have a different set of relevant PSFs as compared to an external task in a harsh environment. A future direction in this sense would be to identify task and environment specific PSFs for the offshore domain for various tasks and environment, collect credible data for these identified PSFs and develop cognitive models based on the collected data. However, such data collection is a monumental task due to various factors such as limitation of resources to perform experiments, the complexity of experiments, and difficulty in setting up high-fidelity experiments. A more practical approach can be the data collection of leading indicators (Tamim et al., 2017). Specifically, leading indicators are used in offshore industry to identify critical events that could lead to high consequence events. Examples of leading indicators include number of standard violations and number of unplanned process changes in the offshore platform. Another approach to counter the difficulty pertaining to lack of data can be the use of newer technologies such as experimental simulations and big-data processing to generate artificial data (Pan et al., 2017). In a general sense, the uncertainty associated with the results deduced from HRA and HRA-based QRA decreases from first-generation methods to third-generation methods. However, the uncertainty associated with HRA and HRA-based QRA can be reduced by appropriate selection of HRA methods and QRA methods that integrate these HRA methods. As a part of this study, it was observed that HRA of offshore emergency/transient scenarios can be effectively carried through first-generation methods: SLIM, HEART, THERP and these HRA methods can be integrated with QRA through FRAM. Similarly, HRA of normal operation scenarios can be achieved through second-generation methods such as SPAR-H and these methods can be integrated with QRA through SBA, BN, and HCL. 3. Conclusion In this study, various HRA methods applied in the offshore domain and QRA methods that facilitate their integration were reviewed from relevant safety journals and conference proceedings. It was observed that first-generation HRA methods were largely applied in the offshore domain for emergency scenarios whereas, second-generation HRA methods were applied for normal design and operation. It was also observed that the HRA based QRA of offshore domains were bound to uncertainties and that these uncertainties can be reduced by implementing appropriate HRA methods or appropriate QRA methods that are integrated with these HRA methods (Fig. 4). Lastly, developing task-specific HRA methods based on cognitive models that derive data from databases relevant to the offshore domain and incorporate various tasks and environmental PSFs can be an ideal path forward in reducing uncertainties associated with HRA and HRA-based QRA. In a general sense, the uncertainty associated with the results deduced from HRA and HRA-based QRA decreases from first-generation methods to third-generation methods. However, the uncertainty associated with HRA and HRA-based QRA can be reduced by appropriate selection of HRA methods and QRA methods that integrate these HRA methods. As a part of this study, it was observed that HRA of offshore emergency/transient scenarios can be effectively carried through first-generation methods: SLIM, HEART, THERP and these HRA methods can be integrated with QRA through FRAM. Similarly, HRA of normal operation scenarios can be achieved through second-generation methods such as SPAR-H and these methods can be integrated with QRA through SBA, BN, and HCL. 3. Conclusion In this study, various HRA methods applied in the offshore domain and QRA methods that facilitate their integration were reviewed from relevant safety journals and conference proceedings. It was observed that first-generation HRA methods were largely applied in the offshore domain for emergency scenarios whereas, second-generation HRA methods were applied for normal design and operation. It was also observed that the HRA based QRA of offshore domains were bound to uncertainties and that these uncertainties can be reduced by implementing appropriate HRA methods or appropriate QRA methods that are integrated with these HRA methods (Fig. 4). Lastly, developing task-specific HRA methods based on cognitive models that derive data from databases relevant to the offshore domain and incorporate various tasks and environmental PSFs can be an ideal path forward in reducing uncertainties associated with HRA and HRA-based QRA. Since first-generation methods provide less importance to task and environmental factors, they are associated with higher HEP estimates with wider uncertainties (Hollnagel, 1989) and therefore, it is important to improve current second-generation methods from the perspective of empirical validation. As second-generation methods essentially deal with cognitive modeling for human-system integration, their empirical validation can be achieved with human experimentation (Blanchard and Fabrycky, 2006). An ideal future direction for research can be the design of human experiments for cognitive models implemented in these methods. Specifically, such methods should deal with the effects of different environmental and task PSFs on various offshore activities. Another approach can be the development of second-generation methods that require reduced quantity of data. An example of such effort is the BN model developed through node reduction algorithms for the application of HRA (Zwirglmaier et al., 2017). The developed model can capture the causal paths in cognitive psychology and therefore provides traceability in HRA. An interesting observation of this review study was identifying that currently, no third-generation methods exist for the offshore domain. Though, it is important to note that only a single third-generation method exists for the nuclear domain as well (Table 2). In a simpler sense, third-generation methods are models that are based on specific human reliability databases and therefore have reduced uncertainties associated with them. However, establishing human reliability databases for an offshore domain that relate multiple PSFs relevant to the offshore domain (Fig. 2) is difficult. Another challenge is that relevant PSFs vary substantially with respect to tasks and environment. A control room task in a normal environment will have a different set of relevant PSFs as compared to an external task in a harsh environment. A future direction in this sense would be to identify task and environment specific PSFs for the offshore domain for various tasks and environment, collect credible data for these identified PSFs and develop cognitive models based on the collected data. However, such data collection is a monumental task due to various factors such as limitation of resources to perform experiments, the complexity of experiments, and difficulty in setting up high-fidelity experiments. A more practical approach can be the data collection of leading indicators (Tamim et al., 2017). Specifically, leading indicators are used in offshore industry to identify critical events that could lead to high consequence events. Examples of leading indicators include number of standard violations and number of unplanned process changes in the offshore platform. Another approach to counter the difficulty pertaining to lack of data can be the use of newer technologies such as experimental simulations and big-data processing to generate artificial data (Pan et al., 2017).
